title: Approved Calculator Mastery & Test Strategy
description: Turn your NCEES-approved calculator into a COGO machine: enter and add angles in DMS, convert DMS‚ÜĒdecimal, and use rectangular‚ÜĒpolar (R‚ĖłP / P‚ĖłR) to solve inverse and forward coordinate geometry in a single keystroke chain ‚ÄĒ then layer on the pacing, guessing, and handbook-search strategy that wins the timed FS exam.
key_topics: The 2026 NCEES approved-calculator list (Casio fx-115/991, HP 33s/35s, TI-30X/36X)
DMS entry, DMS arithmetic, and DMS‚ÜĒdecimal-degree conversion
Rectangular‚ÜĒpolar (R‚ĖłP / P‚ĖłR) as the one-step inverse and forward COGO engine
Inverse: distance and azimuth between two coordinates via R‚ĖłP
Forward: coordinates of a new point from azimuth + distance via P‚ĖłR
Memory registers (STO/RCL/SUM) to chain a traverse without re-keying
Area by coordinates (shoelace / sum of cross-products) on the calculator
Horizontal-curve quickies (T, L) under time pressure
FS time management, flag-and-return, guessing, and efficient handbook search

key_formulas.name: Forward coordinates
key_formulas.formula: N‚āā = N‚āĀ + D¬∑cos(Az);  E‚āā = E‚āĀ + D¬∑sin(Az)

key_formulas.name: Bearing‚Üíazimuth (by quadrant)
key_formulas.formula: NE: Az = ő≤;  SE: Az = 180 ‚ąí ő≤;  SW: Az = 180 + ő≤;  NW: Az = 360 ‚ąí ő≤

content_sections.title: Module Overview
content_sections.content:
## Speed is a skill ‚ÄĒ and it is the cheapest points on the FS

The FS gives you **110 questions in 5 hours 20 minutes ‚ÄĒ about 2.9 minutes each**. The boundary-law, ethics, and definition items are fast if you know them, but the computational questions (Survey Computations and Applied Math, ~27‚Äď41 of the 110) are where time *bleeds*. A surveyor who fumbles a DMS entry, hunts for the polar-conversion key, or hand-cranks an inverse with atan and quadrant bookkeeping will run out of clock before running out of knowledge. A surveyor whose calculator is an extension of their hand banks those points and buys time for the harder items. **Calculator fluency is the single highest-leverage exam skill that costs nothing but practice.**

This module is not new theory ‚ÄĒ Modules 4 and 5 already taught the COGO, traverse, area, and curve formulas. Here you learn to **execute** them at speed on an **NCEES-approved calculator**, and to spend the time you save wisely with deliberate test strategy.

## The 2026 approved-calculator list

NCEES limits you to a fixed list, **re-issued every year** (same list for FS, FE, PS, PE). **No programmable, graphing, or CAS calculators.** For **2026**:

- **Casio** ‚ÄĒ all **fx-115** and **fx-991** models (the name must contain "fx-115" or "fx-991").
- **Hewlett Packard** ‚ÄĒ **HP 33s** and **HP 35s** only (no other HP).
- **Texas Instruments** ‚ÄĒ all **TI-30X** and **TI-36X** models (the name must contain "TI-30X" or "TI-36X").

**Buy and master one of two:** the **TI-36X Pro** (the most-recommended FS/FE calculator ‚ÄĒ DMS, polar‚ÜĒrectangular, matrices, stats, solver, two-line MathPrint display) or the **Casio fx-991EX ClassWiz** (fast, large display, excellent DMS/stat, TABLE mode). The HP 33s/35s are **discontinued** and use RPN ‚ÄĒ great for COGO chaining if you already love RPN, but not worth learning from scratch. **Always confirm the current year's list on ncees.org before your exam**, and bring a **second approved calculator** as a backup.

content_sections.title: Key Concepts
content_sections.content:
## The five operations every surveyor must own

Master these and you can solve almost every FS computational item quickly:

1. **DMS entry & arithmetic** ‚ÄĒ type, add, and subtract angles in degrees-minutes-seconds.
2. **DMS ‚ÜĒ decimal degrees** ‚ÄĒ convert in one keystroke, both directions.
3. **Rectangular ‚Üí polar (R‚ĖłP)** ‚ÄĒ turns (őĒE, őĒN) into (distance, angle): the **inverse** in one step.
4. **Polar ‚Üí rectangular (P‚ĖłR)** ‚ÄĒ turns (distance, azimuth) into (őĒE, őĒN): the **forward** in one step.
5. **Memory registers** ‚ÄĒ STO/RCL (and SUM where available) to chain a traverse without re-keying coordinates.

## DMS entry, arithmetic, and conversion

Survey angles are **degrees-minutes-seconds** (e.g., 257¬į44‚Ä≤10‚Ä≥). Both approved families convert and compute in DMS natively, so you never have to hand-convert.

- **TI-36X Pro:** the angle key is **[2nd][DMS]** (the `¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥` menu). Enter `257¬į44‚Ä≤10‚Ä≥` as `257 [2nd][DMS]¬į  44 [2nd][DMS]‚Ä≤  10 [2nd][DMS]‚Ä≥`. To **convert a decimal to DMS**, compute the value then `[2nd][DMS] ‚Üí ‚ĖłDMS`. To go **DMS ‚Üí decimal**, just enter the DMS value and the calculator already stores it as decimal internally.
- **Casio fx-991EX:** the key is **[¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥]**. Enter `257 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] 44 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] 10 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥]`. Pressing **[¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥]** again (or `SHIFT [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥]` ‚Üí `‚ĖłDMS`) toggles the **answer** between decimal degrees and D¬įM‚Ä≤S‚Ä≥.
- **HP-33S/35S:** store angles as **H.MMSSss** and convert with **‚ÜíHR** (HMS‚Üídecimal) and **‚ÜíHMS** (decimal‚ÜíHMS).
- **Arithmetic in DMS:** you can add/subtract angles directly ‚ÄĒ e.g., balancing interior angles, or summing a deflection. Enter each angle in DMS and press `+`/`‚ąí`; the result displays in DMS (or convert as needed). Keep the calculator in **DEGREE (DEG) mode** so all trig uses degrees.

## Why R‚ĖłP / P‚ĖłR is the heart of COGO

A survey leg has two equivalent descriptions: **rectangular** (őĒE, őĒN) = (departure, latitude), and **polar** (distance, direction). The calculator's built-in conversions move between them **in one step**, replacing a whole chain of `‚ąö`, `atan`, and quadrant logic:

- **Inverse** (two coordinates ‚Üí distance + azimuth): compute `őĒE = E‚āā ‚ąí E‚āĀ` and `őĒN = N‚āā ‚ąí N‚āĀ`, feed them to **R‚ĖłP**, and read **distance** and **azimuth** directly. The calculator's R‚ĖłP returns the angle measured from the x-axis with the correct sign ‚ÄĒ combine with the handbook quadrant constant (or use `atan2`) to land in 0‚Äď360¬į azimuth.
- **Forward** (point + azimuth + distance ‚Üí new coordinates): feed **distance** and **azimuth** to **P‚ĖłR** and read **őĒE (departure)** and **őĒN (latitude)** directly, then add to the start point. This *is* `őĒN = D¬∑cos(Az)`, `őĒE = D¬∑sin(Az)` done in one operation.

**Convention used here (matches the RPLS notes):** treat **x = őĒE (Easting/departure)** and **y = őĒN (Northing/latitude)**, with the angle measured as an **azimuth from north**. Some calculators define the polar angle from the x-axis; if yours does, enter őĒN as x and őĒE as y (or swap the read-out) so the angle comes out as an azimuth. Drill *your own* unit until you know which slot is which without thinking.

## Angle modes ‚ÄĒ keep it in DEG

Set the calculator to **DEGREE (DEG)** mode and confirm the **DEG** indicator before any trig. A calculator left in **RAD** or **GRAD** is the classic silent blunder ‚ÄĒ answers will be wildly wrong with no error message. Curve, traverse, and inverse work all assume degrees.

## Memory registers ‚ÄĒ chain a traverse

Use **STO/RCL** (TI: `[sto‚Üí]`/`[rcl]`; Casio: `[STO]`/`[RCL]` with variables A,B,C,‚Ä¶,X,Y,M) to hold running Northing and Easting totals so you never re-key a coordinate. On the TI-30Xa the **[SUM+]** key accumulates a value into memory ‚ÄĒ the basis of the leg-by-leg traverse routine in Part B. Store a constant like the vertical-curve parabola term or a fixed radius once, then reuse it. **Every value you re-key is a chance for a blunder; store it instead.**

## Fractions, reciprocals, and other time-savers

- **Reciprocal `x‚ĀĽ¬Ļ`** reads a scale denominator (e.g., `f/(H‚ąíh)` then `x‚ĀĽ¬Ļ`) or converts relative precision to a 1:N ratio.
- **Fraction entry** (TI MathPrint, Casio Natural display) lets you type `(A‚āĀ+A‚āā)/2` as a stacked fraction, avoiding order-of-operations slips.
- **Ans** recalls the last result to chain calculations; **parentheses** protect order of operations in long expressions.
- **Stat mode** (mean, sample ŌÉ) handles repeated-measurement and error-propagation items without summing by hand.

content_sections.title: Essential Formulas
content_sections.content:
## Each survey task ‚Üí the calculator operation that solves it

| Survey task | Math | Calculator operation |
|---|---|---|
| Convert a field angle to decimal | `dec = D + M/60 + S/3600` | enter in **DMS**, read decimal (toggle) |
| Convert a decimal answer to DMS | ‚ÄĒ | **‚ĖłDMS** (TI `[2nd][DMS]`, Casio `SHIFT[¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥]`) |
| Add/subtract angles | balance / deflection sums | enter each in **DMS**, `+` / `‚ąí` |
| **Inverse** (two pts ‚Üí distance) | `D = hypot(őĒN, őĒE)` | **R‚ĖłP**, read r |
| **Inverse** (two pts ‚Üí azimuth) | `Az = (toDeg(atan2(őĒE,őĒN)) + 360) mod 360` | **R‚ĖłP**, read őł, then quadrant constant |
| **Forward** (az+dist ‚Üí coords) | `őĒN = D¬∑cos(Az)`, `őĒE = D¬∑sin(Az)` | **P‚ĖłR**, read őĒE and őĒN |
| Bearing ‚Üí azimuth | quadrant rule (below) | mental + DMS |
| Azimuth ‚Üí bearing | quadrant rule (below) | mental + DMS |
| Back azimuth | `Az_back = (Az + 180) mod 360` | `¬Ī180` |
| Area from coordinates | `A = ¬Ĺ¬∑|ő£(XŠĶĘ¬∑Y_{i+1} ‚ąí X_{i+1}¬∑YŠĶĘ)|` | sum of cross-products, √∑2, abs |
| Curve tangent | `T = R¬∑tan(I/2)` | DEG mode, `tan(I/2)` |
| Curve length | `L = R¬∑I¬∑(ŌÄ/180)` | `R √ó I √ó ŌÄ √∑ 180` or `(I/D)¬∑100` |

## Bearing ‚ÜĒ azimuth quadrant rules (memorize)

Azimuth runs **0‚Äď360¬į clockwise from north**. A bearing is a **quadrant letter + acute angle ő≤**:

| Quadrant | Bearing ‚Üí Azimuth | Azimuth ‚Üí Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| **NE** (Az 0‚Äď90) | `Az = ő≤` | `N ő≤ E` |
| **SE** (Az 90‚Äď180) | `Az = 180 ‚ąí ő≤` | `S (180‚ąíAz) E` |
| **SW** (Az 180‚Äď270) | `Az = 180 + ő≤` | `S (Az‚ąí180) W` |
| **NW** (Az 270‚Äď360) | `Az = 360 ‚ąí ő≤` | `N (360‚ąíAz) W` |

The **sign of őĒN and őĒE** fixes the quadrant on an inverse: őĒN+/őĒE+ ‚Üí NE; őĒN‚ąí/őĒE+ ‚Üí SE; őĒN‚ąí/őĒE‚ąí ‚Üí SW; őĒN+/őĒE‚ąí ‚Üí NW.

## Inverse ‚ÄĒ the azimuth, robustly

The one-line bare-helper form that always lands in 0‚Äď360¬į:

`Az = (toDeg(atan2(őĒE, őĒN)) + 360) ‚ąí 360¬∑floor((toDeg(atan2(őĒE, őĒN)) + 360)/360)`

Note the argument order **atan2(őĒE, őĒN)** ‚ÄĒ easting first, northing second ‚ÄĒ because azimuth is measured from north (the őĒN axis). On the calculator, R‚ĖłP does this for you; the formula is the fallback when you key it by hand.

## DMS as total seconds (handy for drills/precision)

`total seconds = D¬∑3600 + M¬∑60 + S`, and back: `D = floor(sec/3600)`, `M = floor((sec mod 3600)/60)`, `S = sec mod 60`.

## Combined-factor and other one-key chains

- **Grid from ground:** `grid = ground √ó combined factor`; **ground from grid:** `ground = grid / combined factor`. Store the combined factor, multiply/divide.
- **Scale denominator:** compute the ratio, press `x‚ĀĽ¬Ļ`.
- **Relative precision:** `misclosure √∑ perimeter`, then `x‚ĀĽ¬Ļ` for 1:N.

content_sections.title: Worked Examples
content_sections.content:
## Keystroke notation

`[2nd]`, `[SHIFT]`, `[¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥]`, `[R‚ĖłP]`, `[P‚ĖłR]`, `[x‚áĄy]`, `[‚ĖłDMS]` are calculator keys. **Always confirm DEGREE (DEG) mode first.** Where the TI and Casio differ, both are shown; HP-33S/35S (RPN) is noted where the Part-B resources give it.

<svg width="460" height="300" viewBox="0 0 460 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="background:#fff;border:1px solid #ccc">
  <!-- axes: x = E (departure), y = N (latitude) -->
  <line x1="60" y1="250" x2="420" y2="250" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="110" y1="280" x2="110" y2="30" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="425" y="254" font-size="12">+E (departure, x)</text>
  <text x="86" y="26" font-size="12">+N (latitude, y)</text>
  <!-- leg from P1 to P2 -->
  <circle cx="110" cy="250" r="4" fill="#1c4e80"/>
  <text x="82" y="266" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">P1</text>
  <circle cx="330" cy="110" r="4" fill="#c0392b"/>
  <text x="338" y="108" font-size="12" fill="#c0392b">P2</text>
  <line x1="110" y1="250" x2="330" y2="110" stroke="#c0392b" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="205" y="170" font-size="12" fill="#c0392b">D (r)</text>
  <!-- delta E and delta N legs -->
  <line x1="110" y1="250" x2="330" y2="250" stroke="#1a5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <text x="200" y="266" font-size="12" fill="#1a5">őĒE</text>
  <line x1="330" y1="250" x2="330" y2="110" stroke="#1a5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <text x="336" y="185" font-size="12" fill="#1a5">őĒN</text>
  <!-- azimuth arc from north -->
  <path d="M 110 200 A 50 50 0 0 1 145 165" fill="none" stroke="#7a5a2a" stroke-width="1.3"/>
  <text x="120" y="180" font-size="12" fill="#7a5a2a">Az</text>
  <text x="150" y="292" font-size="11" fill="#555">R‚ĖłP: (őĒE, őĒN) ‚Üí (D, Az)   ¬∑   P‚ĖłR: (D, Az) ‚Üí (őĒE, őĒN)</text>
</svg>

---

## Example 1 ‚ÄĒ Enter and ADD two angles in DMS

Add interior angles **48¬į17‚Ä≤35‚Ä≥** and **63¬į42‚Ä≤50‚Ä≥** (e.g., checking an angle sum).

**TI-36X Pro:** `48 [2nd][DMS]¬į 17 [2nd][DMS]‚Ä≤ 35 [2nd][DMS]‚Ä≥  +  63 [2nd][DMS]¬į 42 [2nd][DMS]‚Ä≤ 50 [2nd][DMS]‚Ä≥  =` ‚Üí `112.0069¬į`; press `[2nd][DMS] ‚ĖłDMS` ‚Üí **112¬į00‚Ä≤25‚Ä≥**.

**Casio fx-991EX:** `48 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] 17 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] 35 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] + 63 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] 42 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] 50 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] =` ‚Üí toggles to **112¬į00‚Ä≤25‚Ä≥** (press `[¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥]` to switch the answer form).

**Check:** 35‚Ä≥ + 50‚Ä≥ = 85‚Ä≥ = 1‚Ä≤25‚Ä≥; 17‚Ä≤ + 42‚Ä≤ + 1‚Ä≤ = 60‚Ä≤ = 1¬į; 48¬į + 63¬į + 1¬į = **112¬į00‚Ä≤25‚Ä≥**. ‚úď Subtraction works identically with `‚ąí` (used for angle balancing).

---

## Example 2 ‚ÄĒ Convert DMS ‚ÜĒ decimal degrees

Convert **257¬į44‚Ä≤10‚Ä≥** to decimal, then convert **330.5¬į** to DMS.

1. **DMS ‚Üí decimal.** `dec = 257 + 44/60 + 10/3600 = 257 + 0.733333 + 0.002778 = 257.7361¬į`.
   - **TI-36X Pro:** enter `257 [2nd][DMS]¬į 44 ‚Ä≤ 10 ‚Ä≥  =` ‚Üí reads **257.7361¬į** (already decimal internally).
   - **Casio fx-991EX:** `257 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] 44 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] 10 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] =`, then `[¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥]` to show **257.7361¬į**.
2. **decimal ‚Üí DMS.** `330.5¬į`: 0.5¬į √ó 60 = 30‚Ä≤ ‚Üí **330¬į30‚Ä≤00‚Ä≥**.
   - **TI:** `330.5 [2nd][DMS] ‚ĖłDMS` ‚Üí **330¬į30‚Ä≤00‚Ä≥**. **Casio:** `330.5 = SHIFT[¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥](‚ĖłDMS)` ‚Üí **330¬į30‚Ä≤00‚Ä≥**. **HP-35S:** `330.5 ‚ÜíHMS` ‚Üí 330.3000 (read as 330¬į30‚Ä≤00‚Ä≥).

---

## Example 3 ‚ÄĒ INVERSE between two coordinates via R‚ĖłP (distance + azimuth)

P1 (N 17,095.842, E 44,600.312) and P2 (N 16,917.920, E 43,781.054). Find the distance and azimuth P1‚ÜíP2.

1. **Differences:** `őĒE = E‚āā ‚ąí E‚āĀ = 43,781.054 ‚ąí 44,600.312 = ‚ąí819.258`; `őĒN = N‚āā ‚ąí N‚āĀ = 16,917.920 ‚ąí 17,095.842 = ‚ąí177.922`. Both negative ‚Üí **SW quadrant**.
2. **Distance:** `D = hypot(őĒN, őĒE) = ‚ąö(177.922¬≤ + 819.258¬≤) = ‚ąö(31,656 + 671,184) = ‚ąö702,840 = 838.36 ft`.
3. **R‚ĖłP keystrokes (TI-36X Pro):** `[2nd][R‚ĖłP]` with `(őĒE, őĒN)`: enter `‚ąí819.258 [,] ‚ąí177.922 [)] =`; the `r` field reads **838.36** (distance) and the `őł` field reads the angle. Press `[x‚áĄy]` to read őł.
   - **Casio fx-991EX:** `[SHIFT][+](Pol)`  `Pol(‚ąí819.258 , ‚ąí177.922) =` ‚Üí returns `r = 838.36`, `őł`. (Casio Pol takes (x, y); here x = őĒE, y = őĒN.)
   - **TI-30Xa (Part-B method):** enter `őĒE [2nd][x‚áĄy] őĒN [2nd][R‚ĖłP]` ‚Üí DIST; `[2nd][x‚áĄy]` ‚Üí angle; `[2nd][‚ĖłDMS]` for DMS.
4. **Azimuth:** the bare-helper form `Az = (toDeg(atan2(őĒE, őĒN)) + 360) mod 360 = (toDeg(atan2(‚ąí819.258, ‚ąí177.922)) + 360) mod 360`. atan2(‚ąí819.258, ‚ąí177.922) ‚Üí ‚ąí102.256¬į ‚Üí +360 = 257.744¬į ‚Üí **Az = 257¬į44‚Ä≤37‚Ä≥** (SW: 180¬į + bearing 77¬į44‚Ä≤37‚Ä≥). ‚úď matches the Part-B worked sample (~257¬į44‚Ä≤).

**One inverse = one R‚ĖłP.** No separate ‚ąö, no separate atan, no quadrant table if you trust atan2.

---

## Example 4 ‚ÄĒ Coordinates of a point from azimuth + distance via P‚ĖłR

From P1 (N 5,000.00, E 5,000.00), go **azimuth 330¬į30‚Ä≤00‚Ä≥**, **distance 200.00 ft**. Find the new point.

1. **Latitude/Departure:** `őĒN = D¬∑cos(Az) = 200¬∑cos(330.5¬į) = 200¬∑0.87036 = +174.07`; `őĒE = D¬∑sin(Az) = 200¬∑sin(330.5¬į) = 200¬∑(‚ąí0.49242) = ‚ąí98.48` (NW: őĒN+, őĒE‚ąí). ‚úď matches Part-B sample.
2. **P‚ĖłR keystrokes (TI-36X Pro):** `[2nd][P‚ĖłR]`  `P‚ĖłR(200 , 330.5) =` ‚Üí returns the two rectangular components; one is őĒE = **‚ąí98.48**, press `[x‚áĄy]` for őĒN = **+174.07**.
   - **Casio fx-991EX:** `[SHIFT][‚ąí](Rec)`  `Rec(200 , 330.5) =` ‚Üí `x = +174.07, y = ‚ąí98.48` (Casio Rec returns (x along the polar axis); map x‚ÜíőĒN, y‚ÜíőĒE under the north-azimuth convention ‚ÄĒ confirm on your unit).
   - **TI-30Xa (Part-B method):** `200 [2nd][x‚áĄy] 330.5 [2nd][P‚ĖłR]` ‚Üí DEP (őĒE); `[2nd][x‚áĄy]` ‚Üí LAT (őĒN).
3. **New coordinates:** `N‚āā = 5,000.00 + 174.07 = 5,174.07`; `E‚āā = 5,000.00 ‚ąí 98.48 = 4,901.52`.

**P2 ‚Čą (N 5,174.07, E 4,901.52).** One P‚ĖłR replaces two trig calls.

---

## Example 5 ‚ÄĒ Bearing ‚ÜĒ azimuth

(a) Convert bearing **S 36¬į15‚Ä≤ W** to azimuth. SW quadrant ‚Üí `Az = 180¬į + ő≤ = 180¬į + 36¬į15‚Ä≤ =` **216¬į15‚Ä≤00‚Ä≥**.

(b) Convert azimuth **295¬į40‚Ä≤** to a bearing. NW quadrant (270‚Äď360) ‚Üí `bearing = N (360¬į ‚ąí Az) W = N (360¬į ‚ąí 295¬į40‚Ä≤) W = N 64¬į20‚Ä≤ W`.

**Keystrokes:** do the `180 +`, `360 ‚ąí` in **DMS**: e.g., TI `180 [2nd][DMS]¬į + 36 ¬į 15 ‚Ä≤ =` ‚Üí **216¬į15‚Ä≤00‚Ä≥**; Casio `360 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] ‚ąí 295 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] 40 [¬į‚Ä≤‚Ä≥] =` ‚Üí **64¬į20‚Ä≤00‚Ä≥**. **Back azimuth** is just `Az ¬Ī 180`: 216¬į15‚Ä≤ ‚Üí 36¬į15‚Ä≤; 64¬į20‚Ä≤ ‚Üí 244¬į20‚Ä≤.

---

## Example 6 ‚ÄĒ AREA by coordinates (sum of cross-products)

Four corners in order ‚ÄĒ A(N 1,000, E 1,000), B(N 1,000, E 1,400), C(N 1,300, E 1,400), D(N 1,300, E 1,000) ‚ÄĒ using **X = E, Y = N**.

1. **Cross-products** `ő£(XŠĶĘ¬∑Y_{i+1} ‚ąí X_{i+1}¬∑YŠĶĘ)`:
   - A‚ÜíB: 1000¬∑1000 ‚ąí 1400¬∑1000 = ‚ąí400,000
   - B‚ÜíC: 1400¬∑1300 ‚ąí 1400¬∑1000 = +420,000
   - C‚ÜíD: 1400¬∑1300 ‚ąí 1000¬∑1300 = +520,000
   - D‚ÜíA: 1000¬∑1000 ‚ąí 1000¬∑1300 = ‚ąí300,000
   - ő£ = +240,000
2. **Area:** `A = ¬Ĺ¬∑|240,000| = 120,000 ft¬≤` ‚Üí √∑ 43,560 = **2.755 acres** (a 400 √ó 300 rectangle ‚úď).

**Keystrokes:** chain it on one line with parentheses, or store each `XŠĶĘ¬∑Y_{i+1} ‚ąí X_{i+1}¬∑YŠĶĘ` with `[SUM+]`/`+` into memory, then `[RCL] √∑ 2 [x‚áĄy]`. On the **HP-33S/35S** the stored **AREA** routine accepts sequential N,E and returns acreage directly. **Tip:** for a quick rectangle, don't run a full routine ‚ÄĒ just multiply the sides.

---

## Example 7 ‚ÄĒ Horizontal-curve quickie (T and L)

R = 1,145.92 ft, deflection I = 24¬į00‚Ä≤00‚Ä≥. Find T and L fast.

1. **Tangent:** `T = R¬∑tan(I/2) = 1145.92¬∑tan(12¬į) = 1145.92¬∑0.212557 = 243.57 ft`.
2. **Length:** `L = R¬∑I¬∑(ŌÄ/180) = 1145.92¬∑(24¬∑ŌÄ/180) = 1145.92¬∑0.418879 = 480.00 ft` (or `(I/D)¬∑100` with D = 5729.58/R = 5.000¬į ‚Üí (24/5)¬∑100 = 480.00 ‚úď).

**Keystrokes (TI-36X Pro, DEG):** `1145.92 √ó tan( 24 √∑ 2 ) =` ‚Üí 243.57; `1145.92 √ó 24 √ó ŌÄ √∑ 180 =` ‚Üí 480.00. **Casio fx-991EX:** identical. **Time-saver:** when D is given, `(I/D)¬∑100` skips ŌÄ entirely. Type `I/2` directly into `tan(` ‚ÄĒ don't compute I/2 separately.

content_sections.title: Exam Tips & Calculator Strategy
content_sections.content:
## NCEES calculator rules (don't get turned away at the door)

- Your calculator **must be on the current approved list** (2026: Casio fx-115/fx-991, HP 33s/35s, TI-30X/36X). **No programmable, graphing, or CAS** models. The proctor checks it.
- **Clear all memory** before the exam if your model can store programs/notes ‚ÄĒ NCEES expects a cleared calculator. Stored COGO programs from the HP program books are **not** allowed on the actual exam (they were study/field references, not test tools).
- Bring a **second approved calculator** and **fresh batteries** (or solar + light). A dead calculator mid-exam is catastrophic and avoidable.
- **Confirm the list on ncees.org for your exam year** ‚ÄĒ it is re-issued annually and a model can drop off.

## Calculator discipline that prevents blunders

- **DEG mode, always** ‚ÄĒ verify the DEG indicator before any trig. RAD/GRAD is the silent killer.
- **Store, don't re-key.** Use STO/RCL for running N/E totals and reused constants; every re-typed number is a blunder risk.
- **One inverse, one R‚ĖłP; one forward, one P‚ĖłR.** Don't hand-crank ‚ąö + atan + quadrant ‚ÄĒ let the conversion do it, and trust atan2 for the quadrant.
- **Sanity-check the quadrant** from the signs of őĒN, őĒE (NE/SE/SW/NW). If a point can't be SW of a known point, an azimuth near 200¬į is wrong.
- **Round only at the end.** Carry full precision; format with ‚ĖłDMS or fixed decimals last.
- **Use the simplest method.** Don't run a traverse routine to area a rectangle (Part D guidance) ‚ÄĒ multiply the sides.

## Time management & test strategy (from the Part-D strategies)

- **Pace ‚Čą 2.9 min/question** (110 in 5 h 20 min). Keep momentum, not raw speed.
- **7-step flow:** (1) preview the exam, (2) answer the **easiest first**, (3) **flag and skip** hard ones, (4) calculate, (5) **eliminate** wrong answers, (6) **educated guess**, (7) use leftover time to **review**.
- **Bank the fast points first:** boundary-law, ethics, and definition items are quick if known ‚ÄĒ don't let a 6-minute COGO problem early in the exam starve them of time.
- **Read the whole problem before keying anything** ‚ÄĒ don't enter every given coordinate until you know which corners/lines the question actually needs (Part-D calculation discipline). Solve each inverse/value **once** and store it.
- **Make a margin sketch** (vicinity sketch) for COGO/traverse/curve items ‚ÄĒ it prevents quadrant and PC/PT stationing errors.

## Guessing & flag-and-return

- **There is no penalty for wrong answers** ‚ÄĒ **never leave a question blank.** Answer every one before time expires.
- **Eliminate with strike-throughs**, then guess among what's left; watch for **distracters** that are too broad/narrow or off by a unit (ft vs. m, 27 vs. 9, 5729.58 vs. 5730).
- **Clues hide in other questions' preambles** ‚ÄĒ a definition or value you need may appear elsewhere.
- **Flag-and-return:** mark long multi-step items, move on, and come back with the time you banked from the quick ones. **Trust your gut** on split decisions; change an answer only with a concrete reason.

## Using the electronic FS Reference Handbook efficiently

- The handbook (**v2.5**) and the standards PDFs are searchable and open the **entire exam** ‚ÄĒ but the handbook is **big**. **Know where each formula lives** (DMS/trig, Inverse & quadrant constant, Horizontal/Vertical curves, Area formulas, Earthwork, Photogrammetry, Probability & statistics) so you **confirm**, not hunt.
- **Practice with the actual PDF from MyNCEES** before exam day; learn the search terms that jump to each section.
- The handbook gives the **inverse quadrant constant C** (0/180/180/360) and the curve formulas verbatim ‚ÄĒ pair them with your R‚ĖłP/P‚ĖłR muscle memory so the calculator does the arithmetic and the handbook only confirms the form.

## Common traps

- Calculator in **RAD** instead of DEG. ¬∑ Mixing **őĒE/őĒN order** into atan2 (azimuth uses atan2(őĒE, őĒN)). ¬∑ Reading **r** when you wanted **őł** (or vice versa) after R‚ĖłP ‚ÄĒ press `[x‚áĄy]`. ¬∑ Forgetting the **quadrant constant** so a SW azimuth reads as NE. ¬∑ `PT = PC + L` (arc), **not** PC + 2T. ¬∑ **√∑27** for cubic yards, **√∑43,560** for acres ‚ÄĒ don't swap. ¬∑ Leaving an item **blank** (always guess).

questions.question_text: Which calculator operation most directly gives the distance AND azimuth between two known coordinates?
questions.explanation: Feeding őĒE and őĒN into R‚ĖłP returns the distance (r) and direction (őł) in one step ‚ÄĒ exactly the inverse. P‚ĖłR does the reverse (forward) computation; the other modes are unrelated.

questions.question_text: Which calculator operation most directly gives the coordinates of a new point from a known azimuth and distance?
questions.explanation: P‚ĖłR turns (distance, azimuth) into rectangular components (őĒE, őĒN), which you add to the start point. This is the forward computation őĒN = D¬∑cos(Az), őĒE = D¬∑sin(Az) in one operation.

questions.question_text: Before doing any trigonometric (curve, inverse, traverse) computation on an approved calculator, you should verify the calculator is in:
questions.explanation: Survey angles are in degrees, so the calculator must be in DEG. A calculator left in RAD or GRAD gives wildly wrong trig results with no error message ‚ÄĒ the classic silent blunder.

questions.question_text: Which calculators are on the NCEES approved list for 2026 exams?
options: Casio fx-115/fx-991, HP 33s/35s, and TI-30X/TI-36X models
TI-84 Plus and Casio fx-9750 graphing models
questions.correct_answer: Casio fx-115/fx-991, HP 33s/35s, and TI-30X/TI-36X models
questions.explanation: The 2026 list permits all Casio fx-115 and fx-991 models, only the HP 33s and HP 35s, and all TI-30X and TI-36X models. No programmable, graphing, or CAS calculators are allowed.

questions.question_text: Under the convention used for COGO on a scientific calculator, which axis represents őĒN (latitude)?
questions.explanation: By convention x = őĒE (Easting/departure) and y = őĒN (Northing/latitude), with the azimuth measured from north. R‚ĖłP and P‚ĖłR map between (őĒE, őĒN) and (distance, azimuth).

questions.question_text: To convert the bearing S 40¬į00‚Ä≤ W to an azimuth you would compute:
questions.explanation: A southwest bearing converts by Az = 180¬į + ő≤. So S 40¬į00‚Ä≤ W ‚Üí 180¬į + 40¬į = 220¬į00‚Ä≤ azimuth.

questions.question_text: The back azimuth of a line whose forward azimuth is 312¬į15‚Ä≤ is:
questions.explanation: Back azimuth = (Az + 180¬į) mod 360¬į = 312¬į15‚Ä≤ ‚ąí 180¬į = 132¬į15‚Ä≤ (subtract 180¬į when Az > 180¬į).

questions.question_text: When entering an inverse on the calculator, the robust one-line azimuth formula uses which argument order for atan2?
options: atan2(őĒE, őĒN) ‚ÄĒ easting first, northing second
atan2(őĒN, őĒE) ‚ÄĒ northing first, easting second
questions.correct_answer: atan2(őĒE, őĒN) ‚ÄĒ easting first, northing second
questions.explanation: Azimuth is measured clockwise from north (the őĒN axis), so the azimuth is atan2(őĒE, őĒN). Reversing the order gives an angle measured from east instead and produces wrong azimuths.

questions.question_text: On a TI-36X Pro / Casio fx-991, after an R‚ĖłP conversion you see the distance but need the angle. You should:
options: press the [x‚áĄy] (swap) key to read őł
questions.correct_answer: press the [x‚áĄy] (swap) key to read őł
questions.explanation: R‚ĖłP returns both r and őł; the swap key (or reading the second output field) displays the angle without re-entering the coordinates.

questions.question_text: Adding the DMS angles 22¬į45‚Ä≤30‚Ä≥ and 17¬į20‚Ä≤40‚Ä≥ gives:
questions.explanation: 30‚Ä≥ + 40‚Ä≥ = 70‚Ä≥ = 1‚Ä≤10‚Ä≥; 45‚Ä≤ + 20‚Ä≤ + 1‚Ä≤ = 66‚Ä≤ = 1¬į06‚Ä≤; 22¬į + 17¬į + 1¬į = 40¬į. Result 40¬į06‚Ä≤10‚Ä≥. The calculator does this directly in DMS mode.

questions.question_text: Why is storing a running coordinate total with STO/RCL preferred over re-typing it on each traverse leg?
options: It avoids re-keying errors (blunders) and saves time
It changes the angle mode automatically
It is required by NCEES rules
It increases the calculator's display precision
questions.correct_answer: It avoids re-keying errors (blunders) and saves time
questions.explanation: Every re-typed number is a chance to introduce a blunder. Storing running Northing/Easting totals (and reused constants) in memory both prevents transcription errors and speeds up leg-by-leg traversing.

questions.question_text: On a single vertical photo problem you compute f/(H‚ąíh) = 1√ó10‚ĀĽ‚Āī and need the scale denominator. The fastest keystroke is:
options: press the reciprocal x‚ĀĽ¬Ļ key to read 10,000
questions.correct_answer: press the reciprocal x‚ĀĽ¬Ļ key to read 10,000
questions.explanation: Scale is a ratio; pressing x‚ĀĽ¬Ļ turns 1√ó10‚ĀĽ‚Āī into 10,000, i.e., a scale of 1:10,000. The reciprocal key is also handy for relative-precision 1:N ratios.

questions.question_text: Which statement about programmable/graphing calculators on the FS exam is correct?
options: They are prohibited; only models on the NCEES approved list may be used
Any calculator is allowed if its memory is cleared
Graphing calculators are allowed if they are not used for graphing
Programmable calculators are allowed if the programs are survey-related
questions.correct_answer: They are prohibited; only models on the NCEES approved list may be used
questions.explanation: NCEES bans programmable, graphing, and CAS calculators outright. Only models on the current approved list (Casio fx-115/991, HP 33s/35s, TI-30X/36X) are permitted, and memory should be cleared.

questions.question_text: Approximately how much time per question does the FS allow (110 questions in 5 h 20 min)?
questions.explanation: 320 minutes √∑ 110 questions ‚Čą 2.9 minutes each. Banking quick boundary/ethics/definition points buys time for multi-step COGO problems.

questions.question_text: The best test-taking strategy when you reach a long multi-step COGO problem early in the exam is to:
options: flag it, skip ahead to bank quick points, and return to it later
spend as long as needed before moving on
skip it permanently and leave it blank
questions.correct_answer: flag it, skip ahead to bank quick points, and return to it later
questions.explanation: Flag-and-return preserves momentum: answer the fast questions first, then come back to the time-consuming problem with the time you banked. Never leave it blank ‚ÄĒ there is no penalty for guessing.

questions.question_text: Because the FS has no penalty for wrong answers, you should:
options: answer every question, guessing if necessary, before time expires
leave hard questions blank to avoid lowering your score
answer only the questions you are certain about
randomly fill all answers at the start
questions.correct_answer: answer every question, guessing if necessary, before time expires
questions.explanation: With no penalty for incorrect answers, a blank can only score zero while a guess has a chance. Always answer every question; eliminate wrong options first to improve guess odds.

questions.question_text: The electronic NCEES FS Reference Handbook is most efficiently used during the exam by:
options: knowing in advance where each formula lives so you confirm rather than hunt
reading it cover-to-cover during the exam
ignoring it and working from memory only
searching it for every single question
questions.correct_answer: knowing in advance where each formula lives so you confirm rather than hunt
questions.explanation: The handbook is searchable but large. Practicing with the actual PDF beforehand so you know where the curve, area, inverse, and statistics formulas sit lets you confirm a formula in seconds instead of burning time hunting.

questions.question_text: A surveyor needs to area a simple 60 ft √ó 120 ft rectangle. The most efficient calculator approach is to:
options: run a full coordinate (shoelace) routine on all four corners
enter all corners into a stored AREA program
questions.explanation: Part-D calculation discipline: use the simplest method. A rectangle's area is just side √ó side ‚ÄĒ running a full traverse/area routine wastes time and invites entry errors.

questions.question_text: On the area-by-coordinates (shoelace) method, which variables are conventionally taken as X and Y?
questions.explanation: In the coordinate (shoelace) area method, X is the Easting and Y is the Northing; you sum the cross-products XŠĶĘ¬∑Y_{i+1} ‚ąí X_{i+1}¬∑YŠĶĘ, halve, and take the absolute value.

questions.question_text: Which is the most reliable way to determine the correct quadrant of an inverse azimuth on the calculator?
options: use atan2(őĒE, őĒN), or read the signs of őĒN and őĒE
always use the acute angle from atan
questions.correct_answer: use atan2(őĒE, őĒN), or read the signs of őĒN and őĒE
questions.explanation: atan2 returns the angle in the correct quadrant automatically. Equivalently, the signs of őĒN and őĒE place the line in NE/SE/SW/NW, to which you add the handbook quadrant constant. A bare atan loses the quadrant.

questions.question_text: True or False: For the curve-length quickie, when the degree of curve D is given, L = (I/D)¬∑100 lets you avoid using ŌÄ entirely.
questions.explanation: True. With D known, L = (I/D)¬∑100 gives the arc length directly in feet without ŌÄ or radians ‚ÄĒ a faster keystroke chain than L = R¬∑I¬∑(ŌÄ/180).

questions.question_text: True or False: You may use a calculator with stored COGO programs (e.g., an HP traverse routine) on the FS exam as long as it is an HP 35s.
questions.explanation: False. The HP 35s is on the approved list, but NCEES expects calculator memory to be cleared and prohibits using stored programs/notes during the exam. The HP program books are study/field references, not exam tools.

questions.question_text: Convert 132¬į15‚Ä≤00‚Ä≥ to decimal degrees.
Express 1¬į30‚Ä≤45‚Ä≥ as a total number of seconds.

problem_templates.name: DMS to total seconds (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE ‚ÄĒ Express the angle {{D:f0}}¬į{{M:f0}}‚Ä≤{{S:f0}}‚Ä≥ as a TOTAL number of seconds.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Total seconds = D¬∑3600 + M¬∑60 + S = {{D:f0}}¬∑3600 + {{M:f0}}¬∑60 + {{S:f0}} = {{_answer}} seconds. A fast mental/keystroke drill that underlies DMS arithmetic.

problem_templates.name: DMS + DMS angle sum (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE ‚ÄĒ Add the two angles {{D1:f0}}¬į{{M1:f0}}‚Ä≤{{S1:f0}}‚Ä≥ and {{D2:f0}}¬į{{M2:f0}}‚Ä≤{{S2:f0}}‚Ä≥ and report the SUM in DECIMAL degrees.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Enter both angles in DMS, press +, and the calculator returns the sum; here in decimal it is ({{D1:f0}}+{{M1:f0}}/60+{{S1:f0}}/3600) + ({{D2:f0}}+{{M2:f0}}/60+{{S2:f0}}/3600) = {{_answer}}¬į. Toggle ‚ĖłDMS for the field-book form.

problem_templates.name: Inverse distance via R‚ĖłP (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE (R‚ĖłP) ‚ÄĒ Point A is at (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}) and point B is at (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}). Using rectangular-to-polar, compute the horizontal DISTANCE A‚ÜíB (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Compute őĒN = {{dN:f2}} and őĒE = {{dE:f2}}, feed them to R‚ĖłP, and read the distance r = hypot(őĒN, őĒE) = {{_answer}} ft. One R‚ĖłP replaces the separate ‚ąö and atan.

problem_templates.name: Inverse azimuth via R‚ĖłP (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE (R‚ĖłP) ‚ÄĒ Point A is at (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}) and point B is at (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}). Using rectangular-to-polar, compute the AZIMUTH A‚ÜíB in decimal degrees (0‚Äď360¬į).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Azimuth is measured from north: Az = atan2(őĒE, őĒN) normalized to 0‚Äď360¬į. With őĒE = {{dE:f2}}, őĒN = {{dN:f2}}, Az = {{_answer}}¬į. On the calculator, R‚ĖłP returns this őł directly ‚ÄĒ read it with the swap key.

problem_templates.name: Forward coordinate via P‚ĖłR (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE (P‚ĖłR) ‚ÄĒ From point P (N {{nP:f2}}, E {{eP:f2}}) go azimuth {{az:f2}}¬į a distance of {{dist:f2}} ft. Using polar-to-rectangular, compute the NORTHING of the new point (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Feed distance {{dist:f2}} and azimuth {{az:f2}}¬į to P‚ĖłR; the latitude őĒN = D¬∑cos(Az) = {{dN:f2}} ft. New Northing = {{nP:f2}} + {{dN:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Bearing to azimuth (SW quadrant drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE ‚ÄĒ A line has the bearing S {{beta:f2}}¬į W. Compute its AZIMUTH (decimal degrees, 0‚Äď360¬į).
problem_templates.explanation_template: A southwest bearing converts by Az = 180¬į + ő≤ = 180 + {{beta:f2}} = {{_answer}}¬į. (NE: ő≤; SE: 180‚ąíő≤; SW: 180+ő≤; NW: 360‚ąíő≤.)

problem_templates.name: Area by coordinates (triangle, shoelace drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE ‚ÄĒ A triangular parcel has corners, in order, A (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}), B (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}), and C (N {{nC:f2}}, E {{eC:f2}}). Using the coordinate (shoelace) method, compute the area in SQUARE FEET.

solution_steps_template.title: Sum of cross-products, halved, absolute value

problem_templates.explanation_template: Apply the shoelace sum with X = Easting, Y = Northing around A‚ÜíB‚ÜíC, halve the absolute value: A = ¬Ĺ|ő£(XŠĶĘ¬∑Y_{i+1} ‚ąí X_{i+1}¬∑YŠĶĘ)| = {{_answer}} ft¬≤. Chain the three cross-products with memory/SUM to avoid re-keying.

problem_templates.name: Horizontal-curve quickie ‚ÄĒ tangent T (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE ‚ÄĒ A circular curve has R = {{R:f2}} ft and deflection angle I = {{I:f2}}¬į. In DEGREE mode, compute the tangent distance T (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: In DEGREE mode, type I/2 straight into tan: T = R¬∑tan(I/2) = {{R:f2}}¬∑tan({{I:f2}}¬į/2) = {{_answer}} ft.

mock_questions.question_text: Which single calculator operation most directly returns both the distance and the azimuth between two coordinates?
mock_questions.explanation: R‚ĖłP takes (őĒE, őĒN) and returns (distance r, direction őł) in one step ‚ÄĒ the inverse computation. P‚ĖłR does the reverse.

mock_questions.question_text: To find the coordinates of a new point from a known azimuth and distance, the calculator operation is:
mock_questions.explanation: P‚ĖłR converts (distance, azimuth) to rectangular őĒE and őĒN, which add to the start coordinates ‚ÄĒ the forward computation őĒN = D¬∑cos(Az), őĒE = D¬∑sin(Az).

mock_questions.question_text: Adding 35¬į50‚Ä≤40‚Ä≥ and 12¬į15‚Ä≤30‚Ä≥ in DMS mode gives:
mock_questions.explanation: 40‚Ä≥+30‚Ä≥ = 70‚Ä≥ = 1‚Ä≤10‚Ä≥; 50‚Ä≤+15‚Ä≤+1‚Ä≤ = 66‚Ä≤ = 1¬į06‚Ä≤; 35¬į+12¬į+1¬į = 48¬į. Result 48¬į06‚Ä≤10‚Ä≥ ‚ÄĒ the calculator does this directly in DMS.

mock_questions.question_text: Converting the azimuth 215¬į00‚Ä≤ to a bearing gives:
mock_questions.explanation: 215¬į is in the SW quadrant (180‚Äď270). Bearing = S (Az ‚ąí 180¬į) W = S (215 ‚ąí 180)¬į W = S 35¬į00‚Ä≤ W.

mock_questions.question_text: The single most important calculator setting to verify before any survey trig is:
mock_questions.explanation: Survey angles are in degrees; a calculator in RAD or GRAD produces wildly wrong trig with no warning. Always confirm DEG before curve, inverse, or traverse work.

mock_questions.question_text: Computing area from corner coordinates by the shoelace method, the cross-product to accumulate for each edge i is:
mock_questions.explanation: Area = ¬Ĺ¬∑|ő£(XŠĶĘ¬∑Y_{i+1} ‚ąí X_{i+1}¬∑YŠĶĘ)| with X = Easting, Y = Northing. Accumulate each edge's cross-product, halve, take the absolute value.

mock_questions.question_text: A circular curve has R = 600 ft and I = 30¬į. Its tangent distance T = R¬∑tan(I/2) is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: T = 600¬∑tan(15¬į) = 600¬∑0.26795 = 160.77 ft. Type I/2 straight into tan in DEGREE mode.

mock_questions.question_text: On the FS exam (110 questions in 5 h 20 min), the recommended pacing strategy is to:
options: answer easy questions first, flag-and-skip hard ones, and return later
solve questions strictly in order regardless of difficulty
spend equal fixed time on every question
do all computational questions first, in full, before any others
mock_questions.correct_answer: answer easy questions first, flag-and-skip hard ones, and return later
mock_questions.explanation: At ~2.9 min/question, banking the quick boundary/ethics/definition points and flagging long COGO problems preserves momentum and protects time for multi-step items.

mock_questions.question_text: Which calculator is NOT permitted on the FS exam under the 2026 approved list?
mock_questions.explanation: Graphing (and programmable/CAS) calculators like the TI-84 are prohibited. The fx-991EX, TI-36X Pro, and HP 35s are all on the approved list.

mock_questions.question_text: After an R‚ĖłP conversion shows the distance, the angle (azimuth) is read by:
options: pressing the [x‚áĄy] swap key (or reading the őł field)
mock_questions.correct_answer: pressing the [x‚áĄy] swap key (or reading the őł field)
mock_questions.explanation: R‚ĖłP outputs both r and őł; the swap key (or the second result field) shows the angle without re-keying the inputs.

mock_questions.question_text: Because the FS imposes no penalty for wrong answers, the correct exam behavior is to:
options: answer every question, guessing among remaining choices if unsure
answer only questions you are fully confident about
guess all answers at the start to save time
mock_questions.correct_answer: answer every question, guessing among remaining choices if unsure
mock_questions.explanation: A blank scores zero; a guess (especially after eliminating wrong options) has a chance. Never leave a question unanswered.

mock_questions.question_text: The most efficient way to use the searchable electronic FS Reference Handbook during the exam is to:
options: know in advance where each formula section lives so you confirm, not hunt
read it fully during the exam
memorize it and never open it
search it from scratch for every question
mock_questions.correct_answer: know in advance where each formula section lives so you confirm, not hunt
mock_questions.explanation: The handbook is large but searchable; practicing with the real PDF so you know where curves, area, inverse, and statistics formulas sit turns a confirmation into seconds rather than a time-consuming hunt.

mock_questions.question_text: The back azimuth of a line with forward azimuth 78¬į30‚Ä≤ is:
To area a simple rectangular lot quickly, the best practice is to:
options: multiply the two side lengths directly rather than run a full COGO/area routine
always run the stored AREA program
convert each corner with R‚ĖłP first
compute the diagonal and use Heron's formula
mock_questions.correct_answer: multiply the two side lengths directly rather than run a full COGO/area routine
mock_questions.explanation: Calculation discipline: use the simplest method. A rectangle is side √ó side; running a full coordinate routine wastes time and invites entry errors.
